items.title: Now that the World Cup is over, can host cities turn their investment into a lasting social impact?
items.summary: The 2026 World Cup has been a real moneymaker for FIFA. Revenue from TV broadcasting rights, ticket sales and other commercial sources is projected to amount to nearly $11 billion, which would make it the most financially successful event in the international soccer governing body's history.

items.title: Children and monkeys share common understanding of geometry
items.summary: With their ability to use tools, remember faces and even learn how to communicate with humans through sign language, nonhuman primates are considered to be among the smartest creatures on Earth. However, some concepts—geometry, for instance—have traditionally been thought of as beyond the abilities

items.title: What explains gender pay gaps in academia?
items.summary: Gender pay gaps in academia are well documented. The harder question is why they persist. A new study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences tested several leading answers to that question.

items.title: Why do some people who trust in science also believe in scientific conspiracy theories?
items.summary: In a world where trust in institutions is generally declining, trust in science remains relatively high. Yet belief in scientific conspiracy theories, such as hushed-up effects of vaccines or airplane "chemtrails," is also widespread.

items.title: Arctic winter sea ice returns to significant decline, ending the temporary slowdown
items.summary: New analysis shows that a "pause" in Arctic winter sea ice decline seen in the early 2020s has ended following record lows in 2025 and early 2026. The study, led by the University of Southampton in collaboration with the National Oceanography Center (NOC), adds the most recent satellite observations

items.title: NASA, GE Aerospace Work Enables Hybrid-Electric Flight Demonstration
items.summary: An aircraft powered by a megawatt-class hybrid-electric engine developed in collaboration with NASA and built by GE Aerospace, demonstrated flight of an innovation that can inform new generations of fuel-saving aircraft power systems. Mounted to a Saab 340B aircraft, the engine flew at Farnborough I

items.title: Researchers complete new assessments of vulnerable Southern Ocean species
items.summary: Postdoctoral researcher Renato Borras-Chavez's area of study—pinnipeds in the Southern Ocean—often takes him far from the University of Rhode Island campus. Now, the researcher in Sarah Kienle's CEAL Lab is sharing more about declining populations of certain seals and will present on the t

items.title: Japan's first mosasaur premaxilla fossil hints at a new species
items.summary: A research team from Okayama University of Science, Tokyo City University, the Natural History Museum, Kishiwada City (Osaka Prefecture), and other institutions has identified four previously unrecognized fossil bones from a rock containing mosasaur remains collected about 30 years ago in Kishiwada

items.title: There's no right or wrong way to say 'Appalachia'
items.summary: People pronounce the word "Appalachia" differently, and much of that contrast depends on where they live. But no matter how you say it, a Virginia Tech project shows that no one way trumps all. The differences are rooted in historical and community influences.

items.title: Why some human brains can outlast other soft tissues after death
items.summary: Why do some brains survive long after death when most other soft tissue decays? That's the question a research team led by Alexandra Morton-Hayward at the University of Oxford set out to answer.

items.title: Greater nutrient variety can counterintuitively shrink microbial diversity, experiments reveal
items.summary: Microbial communities are considered particularly diverse—in soil, water and the human body. A common assumption in ecology is that the greater the diversity of available nutrients, the more species can coexist. A research team at the University of Tübingen has now shown that the opposite can be tru

items.title: Investing podcasts are super popular. But do they help people learn the market?
items.summary: From game highlights to cold cases to political pontificating, podcasts provide the stage for public conversations in 2026. But are we learning anything from all that chatter? When it comes to investing and playing the markets, the answer seems to be yes. New research from the University of Georgia

items.title: World's first gene edited Culicoides biting midges open door for livestock disease research
items.summary: Scientists have successfully edited the genome of biting midges, opening the door to new research on how these insects transmit disease. Culicoides biting midges are small blood-feeding insects responsible for spreading important vector-borne viruses such as bluetongue virus (BTV), Schmallenberg vir

items.title: Research reveals how human bias shapes social media feeds
items.summary: People are naturally attracted to excitement. According to new Virginia Tech research, the same is true on social media, where feeds are overrun with international travel, skydiving and other experiences that may seem out of the ordinary.

items.title: Study could help event organizers prevent crowd crushes and stampedes
items.summary: McGill researchers have found that dangerous crowd conditions leading to surges and stampedes may not emerge suddenly but instead develop gradually, as repeated physical contact and pushing accumulate and become synchronized among nearby individuals. Recognizing how these conditions arise could help

items.title: Rare sighting reveals Arctic bobtail squid and egg masses in subzero Greenland fjord
items.summary: Marine biologists have observed the Arctic bobtail squid in its natural habitat in the North Atlantic Ocean for the first time, including the rare discovery of a mature female with eggs. Dr Paige Maroni, from The University of Western Australia's School of Biological Sciences, is lead author of

items.title: Microplastic tire pollution found in Brisbane balcony dust
items.summary: Tire particles and potentially toxic tire chemical additives have been found in dust collected from Brisbane residential balconies. University of Queensland researchers examined the balcony dust from homes across inner-city, suburban and near-road settings to see how traffic-related pollution travel

items.title: Shroud of Turin DNA sheds light on its biological history
items.summary: New research has uncovered the complex biological history preserved within DNA traces found on the Shroud of Turin, offering fresh insights into one of the world's most studied and debated historical artifacts.

items.title: Understanding how wild plants handle shifting disease threats, conditions
items.summary: St. Louis is a plant science powerhouse, but outside the neatly tended rows of crops and gleaming greenhouses sits another treasure trove of biological data: The wild plants tucked into yards, sidewalk cracks, ditches and fields have much to reveal. WashU's plant scientists are also among the m

items.title: AI falls short on context and cultural rhetoric in United Nations speech translations
items.summary: As generative artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly used in translation, questions have been raised about whether it could eventually replace professional interpreters. A joint study led by Lingnan University found that while AI can improve translation efficiency, it is still less capable tha

items.title: Exploring the use of accounts to manage reluctant participation in microteaching sessions
items.summary: Classrooms often fall silent when the teacher poses a question. The pause is not always due to a lack of knowledge. Students may be hesitant, unsure, shy or simply unwilling to participate, and these feelings are often shown through subtle cues such as gaze aversion, delayed responses, posture, faci

items.title: Researchers challenge evidence behind high-profile parental alienation study
items.summary: Researchers from Manchester and Canada have shown that the claim by the authors of a study published earlier this year that most of its participants had experienced parental alienating behaviors during childhood is seriously misleading. Claims in the study that people affected by parental alienating

items.title: From Hampton to Mars: How NASA Langley Helped Land on the Red Planet
items.summary: Fifty years ago, NASA’s Viking 1 and 2 landers made the first successful landings on Mars, opening a new era in planetary exploration. Behind that achievement was a team at NASA’s Langley Research Center in Hampton, Virginia, whose steady leadership and technical expertise helped turn an ambitious i

items.title: Red tape and conflicting priorities limit the impact of social businesses, research finds
items.summary: Social businesses are being prevented from delivering lasting benefits to their regions because of complex policies, unclear guidance, financial constraints and poor use of resources, according to new research from the Durham University Business School. Social businesses are organizations that aim t

items.title: Bee brains reveal how colonies divide work without central planning
items.summary: What determines which tasks bees perform in their colony? Biologists from Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf (HHU) and the universities in Cologne and Frankfurt/Main have investigated the role neural activity plays in this process. In an article published in the Proceedings of the National Academy

items.title: Our First View of the Surface of Mars
items.summary: “Touchdown, we have touchdown!” At 5:12 a.m. PDT, July 20, 1976, mission controllers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory erupted in cheers as they learned that the Viking 1 lander had survived its descent through the thin Martian atmosphere. Forty minutes later, the lander’s first image began to app

items.title: Food labeling combined with higher prices for carbon-intensive foods could help reduce UK greenhouse gases by 5%
items.summary: Economists say labeling supermarket food to show its environmental impact, combined with taxing the most carbon-intensive items, could slash UK greenhouse gas emissions by almost 5% while generating net gains for society.

items.title: AI may not make us stupid, but relying on it could weaken the skills built through practice
items.summary: The worry of becoming stupid isn't new; it dates back to the time of the Greek philosopher Socrates, who insisted that writing would destroy memory and weaken the mind. Then came calculators, GPS and computers, all of which again raised concerns that we might forget basic mental skills and lose

items.title: Astronauti su s Mjeseca donijeli kamenje. Još uvijek otkrivamo njihove tajne
items.summary: PRIJE 57 godina, misije Apollo donijele su mjesečevo kamenje koje je otkrilo tajne o postanku Mjeseca i Zemlje. Ti uzorci i danas pokreću znanstvena otkrića i inspiriraju nove misije poput Artemisa.

items.title: Američka obitelj tuži OpenAI: "ChatGPT je potaknuo 29-godišnju majku na samoubojstvo"
items.summary: OBITELJ 29-godišnjakinje koja je počinila samoubojstvo tuži OpenAI. Tvrde da je chatbot GPT-4o poticao njezine religijske zablude i ohrabrivao je da si oduzme život nakon intenzivne komunikacije.

items.title: Obitelj tuži OpenAI. ChatGPT je potaknuo 29-godišnju majku na samoubojstvo
items.summary: OBITELJ 29-godišnjakinje koja je počinila samoubojstvo tuži OpenAI. Tvrde da je chatbot GPT-4o poticao njezine religijske zablude i ohrabrivao je da si oduzme život nakon intenzivne komunikacije.

items.title: Beyond proximity: New policy model to transform access to parks
items.summary: Accessible urban green spaces could save the NHS up to £2.1 billion each year, but current policy fails to ensure parks are truly accessible, research shows. A new policy brief published by the university's Institute for Policy Research (IPR) sets out how a different approach could boost public

items.title: Prescribed burns may be lethal for lizards
items.summary: Scientists are calling for wildlife surveys to be conducted within hours of prescribed burns after new research revealed that evidence of animal deaths can disappear in less than two weeks, significantly underestimating the true toll of fire on Australia's wildlife.

items.title: New machine-learning tool improves accuracy of genomics research
items.summary: University of Virginia School of Medicine scientists have identified a widespread source of error in a popular method for studying the genome and created a machine-learning tool to correct it. The free tool could improve the reliability of both conventional and single-cell data generated using this

items.title: Deep-space CubeSat could stretch solar storm warnings from 15 minutes to 3 hours
items.summary: Space weather warnings could be received hours before Earth is hit rather than minutes with the help of a miniature UK-built instrument soon to be stationed in deep space. MAGIC (MAGnetometer from Imperial College) is part of the European Space Agency's Heliospheric Pioneer for Solar and Interp

items.title: Can you guess your favorite World Cup team based on 'signature' passes?
items.summary: Each World Cup team's passing patterns reveal distinctive "signatures" that show whether a team relies heavily on its star player or focuses its strategy on playing against an opponent's strengths, data shows.

items.title: Archaeological dig reveals full extent of Roman villa with intricate mosaic and bath house
items.summary: Archaeologists have uncovered a rare and intricate polychrome mosaic within the remains of a Roman villa beneath Devon farmland. Made up of thousands of red, white and dark blue-gray cubes, the 12 m2 (129 square feet) mosaic would have been a striking decorative floor feature in the villa when it wa

items.title: Giving bees a probiotic diet could help protect them from extreme temperatures
items.summary: Honeybees do not like cold weather. When temperatures dip, they can become sluggish and unable to fly. But no matter the conditions in the outside world, they try to keep their hives close to a comfortable 34°C–36°C (93°F–97°F). However, extreme weather, both hot and cold, can overwhelm this interna

items.title: Q&A: How do federal taxes impact local schools and services?
items.summary: Increases to the standard federal tax deduction implemented in 2018 were made permanent a year ago, and researchers are beginning to understand the downstream effects on state and local governments. The increased deduction meant many taxpayers received a bigger tax break by taking the standard deduc

items.title: Swiss researchers hoard vast trove of NASA climate data
items.summary: Researchers have copied massive quantities of publicly available climate and environmental data from NASA onto a Swiss supercomputer to train artificial intelligence models—and for safekeeping amid U.S. funding cuts.

items.title: Wealthier North Carolina neighborhoods may be hotspots for insecticide-resistant tiger mosquitoes
items.summary: The choices people make to control mosquitoes in their own backyards may be helping some of those mosquitoes survive the very insecticides meant to kill them, according to two new studies from North Carolina State University. The paper on the emergence and spatial distribution of resistance appears

items.title: Why you let down your guard on ads when scrolling on social media
items.summary: My wife stared at me in shock as I ripped open the package. The FedEx driver hadn't even made it back to his truck as I held the item up overhead like a trophy. Fumbling for words, she asked: "Why on earth did you buy a Speedo … with dinosaur tear-away trunks?"

items.title: Some dinosaurs could rise like giants until they grew too big
items.summary: Two elephant-sized South American sauropods were unusually well built for standing on their hind legs. Digital tests showed that their robust femurs handled the enormous forces better than those of larger species, especially while the dinosaurs were young. The upright pose may have helped them reach

items.title: Where do the world's biggest diamonds come from? Geology now has answers
items.summary: Some of the most extraordinary gems ever found belong to a rare class of diamonds known as CLIPPIRs (Cullinan-like, Large, Inclusion-Poor, Pure, Irregular, Resorbed). They make up less than 1% of all diamonds on Earth and include three of the largest diamonds ever recovered: the Cullinan, at 3,106 c

items.title: How the most widespread parasite on Earth reads its genome
items.summary: A parasite carried by billions of people worldwide often causes harmful infections during pregnancy and in immunocompromised individuals and is a leading infectious cause of blindness in South America. Once it enters the body, it can rapidly multiply, spreading from one cell to the next.

items.title: Weather and human activity impact lake extremophiles
items.summary: The Great Salt Lake is separated by a 20-mile (32-kilometer) causeway into a super-salty north arm and a less-salty south arm, with each hosting distinct communities of extremophiles. These organisms mingle at a gap in the causeway, designed to regulate salinity, that has provided microbiologists wi

items.title: Why AI's environmental footprint won't disappear into orbit
items.summary: AI companies increasingly want to move their operations into space. Elon Musk's SpaceX has applied to launch over a million AI data center satellites and ultimately build them on the moon.

items.title: Earth’s waters are quietly running out of oxygen, scientists warn
items.summary: Oxygen is disappearing from oceans, lakes, rivers, and coastal waters at an alarming rate, threatening aquatic life and weakening the natural processes that help regulate Earth’s climate. Scientists warn that this widespread deoxygenation is closely connected to other major planetary threats, includ

items.title: Worried about booking a holiday abroad? How travel operators can provide reassurance
items.summary: Flying abroad for a summer vacation is not as simple as it used to be. Geopolitical uncertainty has already disrupted flight schedules and fuel prices this year. For many, staying closer to home will seem like a sensible option. And in the U.K., warmer summers could also make staycations much more a

items.title: Fourth-year vet students reveal clues to horse MEED and dog bone cancer
items.summary: Texas Tech University's School of Veterinary Medicine fourth-year students Kayden Tanner and Marshall Mays have both been published in international journals after putting hundreds of hours into their research papers.

items.title: Researchers advance tech that could help scientists detect habitable worlds beyond our solar system
items.summary: Are we alone in the universe? For scientists working on NASA's proposed Habitable Worlds Observatory, that question is no longer purely philosophical. It is increasingly becoming an engineering problem.

items.title: Burnout isn't new: 18th‑century farm records show workers describing the same exhaustion
items.summary: Nearly 40% of Canadian workers say they feel burned out often or always, according to a 2025 survey by Mental Health Research Canada and Canada Life. The term has become part of a broader lexicon of modern work, alongside quiet quitting, hustle culture and grindset and "taskmasking."

items.title: On the hunt for Earth's first complex life
items.summary: How life started on our own planet may not seem as interesting as finding evidence of life on Mars or the icy moons Europa or Enceladus. But the search for the first eukaryotes on Earth remains as important to astrobiology as finding life on a far-flung planet. That's in part because, for 90% o

items.title: Africa's wildfire season is actually getting shorter, scientists think they know why
items.summary: Africa is home to around 70% of the world's burned area—the total area of land scorched by wildfires each year—making it the planet's biggest wildfire hotspot. Yet despite this, satellite observations have revealed an unexpected trend over the past two decades: The amount of land burned ac

items.title: Alzheimer’s breakthrough: Scientists restore two hours of sleep without clearing brain plaques
items.summary: Researchers have uncovered a surprising culprit behind sleep loss in Alzheimer’s disease: the brain’s own immune cells. In mice with amyloid plaques, overactive microglia triggered inflammation that kept the brain from getting enough deep, restorative sleep. Temporarily removing most of these cells

items.title: How to keep the air inside your home clean during wildfires
items.summary: The eerie glow of an orange sun through thick wildfire smoke might be unnerving, but the biggest risk from the haze is something much harder to see.

items.title: Greece braces for year's first heat wave
items.summary: Greece, which was spared the record heat that ravaged most of Europe in June, is finally bracing for its first heat wave of the year.

items.title: Floating igloo leaves France for polar exploration mission
items.summary: A floating research station resembling a giant aluminum igloo on Sunday set out from France to begin a long-term mission in the Arctic that will see it drift with the polar ice in one of the most hostile environments.

items.title: 5.5 magnitude earthquake hits Peru's Andes region and kills at least 6 people
items.summary: A 5.5 magnitude earthquake shook the Andes region of Peru, killing at least six people, injuring more than 30 and displacing 300, authorities said Sunday.

items.title: AI tool reveals climate shifts may have fueled bursts of bird evolution
items.summary: University of Michigan researchers have used an AI tool to demonstrate that birds in the group Passeriformes evolved in rapid evolutionary bursts and that these bursts frequently coincided with climate shifts throughout Earth's history.

items.title: Scientists map how the flu virus rewires the human cell from the inside
items.summary: Researchers at EMBL Hamburg and collaborators at the Leibniz Research Institute for Molecular Pharmacology (FMP) have mapped how the influenza A virus rewires infected human cells in unprecedented detail. To do this, the researchers used a customized experimental workflow to directly observe how pro

items.title: FOTO Ovo je prva slika Marsa iz blizine. Evo priče iza nje
items.summary: EKSPEDICIJA Viking 1, lansirana 20. kolovoza 1975. godine, bila je povijesni trenutak u istraživanju svemira.

items.title: An ordinary laptop solved a problem thought to require a quantum computer
items.summary: A quantum problem once described as impossible for classical computers has now been solved using relatively modest hardware. Researchers used tensor networks to compress the overwhelming wave function created by hundreds of entangled qubits, allowing some calculations to run on a laptop. Their resul

items.title: Scientists discover a protein that protects the brain from Alzheimer’s damage
items.summary: A protein called SORLA may act as a powerful defense against the toxic tau tangles involved in Alzheimer’s disease. Mice with extra SORLA had less tau accumulation, less brain atrophy, and healthier connections between neurons, while mice without it experienced more severe damage. The findings also

items.title: Shaping the Emerald City
items.summary: The hills and basins of Seattle, Washington, have been sculpted by glaciers and modified by humans.

items.title: Underwater drones discover coral reefs off Benin, long presumed dead, teeming with life
items.summary: A coral reef, missing and presumed dead, has been found alive and well off the coast of Benin. Surveys in the 1960s, focused on identifying promising fishing areas, flagged a possible deep-water coral reef but assumed it must be dead. Now, using a modern sonar survey and the Deep Sea Camera System p

items.title: UCLA scientists develop mineral sunscreen without the chalky white cast
items.summary: Scientists have redesigned zinc oxide particles to create a mineral sunscreen that protects against ultraviolet radiation without leaving such a noticeable white cast. The SPF 30 formula could make sunscreen easier and more appealing to use across a wider range of skin tones.

items.title: Scientists found two powerful new ways to destroy forever chemicals
items.summary: Scientists are testing two promising ways to destroy PFAS, the stubborn “forever chemicals” that can accumulate in water and resist normal treatment. One method uses collapsing vapor bubbles to generate extreme heat and reactive molecules, while the other uses cold plasma and rising gas bubbles to p

items.title: Pittsburgh's new romance bookstores sell more than happily‑ever‑afters
items.summary: "I'm looking for a book where …" has become a defining phrase of BookTok, TikTok's massive community of readers and book influencers.

items.title: Generating red blood cell-like cells from canine induced pluripotent stem cells
items.summary: Blood transfusions are crucial in human and veterinary medicine, yet human blood reserves are in constant need of donors, while blood bank systems in veterinary care are nearly nonexistent. This leaves canine transfusions largely reliant on donations from healthy dogs, but securing compatible blood

items.title: Creating a map of coral distribution with environmental DNA
items.summary: To track the impacts of environmental change and enable conservation efforts, we must first understand the composition of our ecosystems. A new online resource provides a comprehensive overview of hard coral distribution across the Ryukyu Islands, based on extensive environmental DNA (eDNA) analysis

items.title: How the sun goes to 'sleep' could reveal future space weather when it wakes
items.summary: Scientists have developed a new way to predict the strength of the sun's next activity cycle up to seven years before it reaches its peak.

items.title: Centuries before farming, hunter-gatherers introduced fish to the Norwegian mountains
items.summary: Archaeologists have uncovered the remains of 7,000-year-old fish traps in the mountains of southern Norway, indicating that fish were intentionally introduced to upland lakes by Mesolithic hunter-fisher-gatherers and showing that human management of nature was not limited to agricultural societies.

items.title: Polish families in Spain navigate cultural identity across generations, study finds
items.summary: Emigration often means a profound life upheaval. What challenges do Polish families face after moving to Spain? A researcher from SWPS University, in collaboration with her doctoral supervisors and a researcher from the University of Almería, analyzed family dynamics between children and their paren

items.title: Shared fandom as undervalued social 'medicine' for division, isolation
items.summary: The state of the American social fabric—enormous, diverse and rent by stark partisan divides—is daunting enough that it can be hard to think about, let alone try to mend. At the Harvard Kennedy School, two social scientists are advancing the proposition that sports fandom, a powerful social phenomen

items.title: Dragonfly aerial fights follow some simple rules
items.summary: Dragonflies are renowned for their remarkable flying abilities. Male dragonflies often compete for territory and mating opportunities, engaging in fascinating in-flight battles. Researchers at Imperial College London and Taipei Zoo recently carried out a study investigating the aerial maneuvers male

items.title: Znanstvenici objasnili zašto je nečija kosa ravna, a nečija kovrčava
items.summary: OBLIK kose ne ovisi o vanjskim faktorima, već o obliku folikula ispod kože.
